summary: Gruber defends Phil Schiller's use of the word 'courage' to describe Apple's decision to remove the iPhone's headphone jack, drawing a direct parallel to Steve Jobs using the same word about refusing Flash on iOS. He argues that the courage lies in choosing a sure-to-be-controversial path over the non-controversial status quo when you believe it's right long-term. He distinguishes the two cases — Flash was genuinely terrible technology, while the headphone jack is merely mediocre — but contends the same principle applies: Apple is nudging the industry toward wireless audio the way it once forced the web off Flash. He notes that most companies avoid controversy by doing the popular-but-wrong thing, and that Apple's willingness to absorb outrage for long-term benefit is genuinely rare.
key_points: Controversial change is asymmetric: only opponents get outraged, while supporters are merely ambivalent, which is why the status quo is so sticky
Apple's Flash refusal was a strong push that forced an entire industry to adopt HTML5; removing the headphone jack is a gentler nudge toward wireless audio adoption
The Flash/HTML5 case was bad vs. good; the headphone jack/AirPods case is meh vs. good — a weaker justification but the same structural principle
Google knowingly supported terrible Flash technology on Android to avoid controversy and gain competitive advantage over iOS
Most companies choose popular-but-wrong over unpopular-but-correct; Apple's willingness to absorb predictable outrage for long-term user benefit is what Schiller meant by courage
money_quotes: When it comes to controversial change of the status quo, it's not like that. Only the people who are opposed to the change get outraged.
Strong feelings against change on one side, and widespread ambivalence on the other. That's why the status quo is generally so slow to change, in fields ranging from politics to technology.
Flash is garbage technology. To this day it's not just bad, it's dangerous.
Flash/HTML5 was bad/good. Analog jack/AirPods is meh/good.
Not supporting Flash on iOS in any way, shape, or form was a strong push. Removing the headphone jack but including both Lightning ear buds and a legacy adapter is a nudge.
Few companies other than Apple make decisions that they know will provoke outrage just because they think it's the right thing to do. Most companies will do the wrong thing to avoid controversy.
We, as a species, are hooked up to focus on the short run, and we're hooked up to seek popularity and avoid criticism.
Choosing to do what you know will be unpopular in the short run but you believe will prove correct in the long run takes courage.
key_insight: True product courage isn't dramatic heroism but the willingness to absorb predictable outrage by killing a popular-but-inferior status quo when you believe the alternative is better for users long-term.
